question: Based on Table 1.2-2 (vacuum CNV) and Table 5.2-4 (materials), does the material selection adequately support the containment design?

nuscale_ch01_0356.title: SRP 9.3.3, Rev 3: Equipment and Floor Drainage SystemII.3 Control of Releases of Radioactive Material to the Environment
nuscale_ch01_0356.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale equipment and floor drainage system meets regulatory criteria for controlling the release of radioactive material to the environment, with no parts of the drain system penetrating the containment barrier. This demonstrates conformance with SRP 9.3.3, Rev 3 requirements, ensuring environmental protection from radioactive releases.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards related to the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases relevant to reactor component materials and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0066.title: Material Referenced
nuscale_ch01_0066.summary: This section lists the topical and technical reports referenced in the NuScale Power Plant Design Certification Application, as detailed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover various aspects such as quality assurance, risk significance, safety classification, accident analysis, and methodology qualifications, and are incorporated by reference into the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR).

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s compliance with regulatory requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, referencing specific ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC conditions, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It explains the types of interface requirements (conceptual design information and COL information items), and describes how COL applicants must address site-specific information and any departures from the certified design. Tables referenced in the section summarize these interfaces and list the required COL information items.

nuscale_ch01_0123.title: Table 1.9-2: Conformance with Regulatory Guides (Continued)
nuscale_ch01_0123.summary: This section of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report summarizes the reactor's conformance with various NRC Regulatory Guides. It details which guides are fully, partially, or not applicable to the NuScale design, providing explanations and references to relevant sections of the report for each criterion. The table ensures transparency regarding regulatory compliance in areas such as quality assurance, seismic design, instrumentation, and power systems.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The section describes how NuScale’s containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum to reduce corrosion, improve leak detection, and minimize heat transfer, which enhances safety and reduces debris. The design relies on passive safety features, such as natural cooling and the elimination of large external coolant loops, resulting in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and improving safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer, and serves as a key barrier to fission product release. The RPV and its components are constructed from materials specified by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design life.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure boundary to prevent the release of fission products and provides structural support for various reactor components. Materials used for the RPV are selected according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design life.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details that these components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III standards, with specific classifications for different piping systems, and references the applicable ASME BPVC edition and regulatory guides used for certification.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s life, in accordance with regulatory definitions. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, emphasizing their roles in maintaining pressure containment and system safety.

nuscale_ch01_0188.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from Aluminum Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0188.summary: The section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding pH control and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion in emergency coolant water for pressurized water reactors. While NuScale’s design lacks certain systems referenced in the criteria (such as containment spray systems and sumps), the intent of the guidelines is still met due to similar water recirculation processes within the containment vessel. Other criteria, such as those related to minimum containment pressure and dual containment leakage, are deemed not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0857.title: SRP 19.1, Rev 3: Determining The Technical Adequacy of Probabilistic Risk Assessment For Risk-Informed License Amendment Requests After Initial Fuel LoadAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0857.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various NRC regulatory criteria related to human factors engineering and probabilistic risk assessment (PRA). It notes that NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report aligns with requirements for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, while partial conformance is noted for PRA and severe accident evaluation, with some responsibilities deferred to COL applicants for site-specific hazards and PRA updates.

nuscale_ch01_0446.title: DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater SystemII.7 Flow Accelerated Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0446.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Condensate and Feedwater System, specifically regarding flow accelerated corrosion, stating that the design fully conforms to requirements in this area. It also notes that certain criteria related to heat removal, inspection, and testing are not applicable to the NuScale design, and that systems such as steam generator blowdown and auxiliary feedwater are not used in the NuScale plant.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, in compliance with technical specifications and based on as-built material properties. It also confirms that the NuScale reactor vessel meets regulatory requirements for pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy throughout its 60-year design life, ensuring vessel integrity under anticipated operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and comply with relevant U.S. regulatory requirements to ensure high quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture resistance. These materials are subject to rigorous testing and quality assurance measures, and detailed specifications are provided in referenced tables and sections of the report.

nuscale_ch01_0694.title: DSRS 15.1.6, Rev 0: Loss of Containment VacuumII.2 Specific CriteriaCladding Integrity
nuscale_ch01_0694.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding radiological consequences of main steam line failures outside containment. It notes partial conformance to certain guidance, as some radiological acceptance criteria and methodologies are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3. Additionally, the section confirms that the specific criterion for reactor coolant pressure under DSRS 15.1.6 is met.

nuscale_ch01_0167.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.1 Primary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0167.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3 requirements for primary containment, as its passive containment vessel does not include fission product clean-up or pressure suppression systems typical of conventional LWRs. Instead, fission product control is achieved through the inherent passive design, with the containment vessel submerged in a reactor pool. Requirements related to secondary containment and other fission product control systems are not applicable, as NuScale does not utilize these features.

nuscale_ch01_0168.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.2 Secondary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0168.summary: The NuScale design does not include secondary containment or fission product clean-up systems typically required for LWRs, as its passive safety features and submerged containment vessel inherently control fission products. Therefore, the regulatory criteria in SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3 related to secondary containment and certain fission product control systems are not applicable to the NuScale design.

edges.description: Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance for equipment and drainage systems, while Section B lists references including material reliability and quality assurance standards, indicating that the material selection in Section B is intended to support the containment design requirements discussed in Section A.

Section A references regulatory criteria and acceptance of design features, while Section B lists the specific topical and technical reports (including material selection and quality assurance) that are incorporated by reference to support those criteria, connecting the regulatory requirements to the supporting documentation.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for equipment and drainage systems, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and boundaries for certified and site-specific design, connecting them through the identification of which systems and components (such as containment materials) are governed by certified design requirements.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and acceptance for system design, while Section B details compliance with ASME code cases for material selection and construction, connecting them through the requirement that material choices (Section B) must satisfy the regulatory criteria for containment design (Section A).

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for systems related to radioactive material control, while Section B defines the interface requirements and boundaries for certified and site-specific design elements, connecting them through the identification of which systems and components (such as containment and materials) are governed by the certified design and thus subject to the regulatory criteria described in Section A.

Section A lists references to industry standards and guidelines relevant to material selection and quality assurance, while Section B identifies the specific topical reports and technical documents incorporated by reference for the NuScale design, thus Section A's references are substantiated and detailed by the documents cataloged in Section B.

Section A provides references to material standards and quality assurance requirements that are relevant for evaluating the adequacy of material selection, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and identifies which structures, systems, and components (including containment) are part of the certified design, thus connecting the material standards in A to the certified containment design in B.

Section A references industry standards and guidelines for material selection and quality assurance, while Section B discusses compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory requirements for design and construction, connecting them through the need for material choices (from A) to satisfy the regulatory and code requirements (in B) for containment integrity.

Section A provides references to material standards and quality assurance requirements that underpin the material selection detailed in Table 5.2-4, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and identifies which structures, systems, and components (such as the vacuum CNV) are part of the certified design as listed in Table 1.2-2, thereby connecting the material selection to the containment design scope.

Section A lists referenced materials and topical reports that provide technical bases for the design, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and identifies which structures, systems, and components (including containment) are part of the certified design, thus connecting the referenced materials to the scope of the containment design.

Section A lists the referenced topical reports and quality assurance program that provide the basis for material selection, while Section B discusses compliance with ASME and NRC requirements for overpressure protection, connecting the adequacy of material selection (from A) to the regulatory design requirements (in B).

Section A lists referenced materials and topical reports that provide technical bases for the design, while Section B outlines the interface requirements and identifies which structures, systems, and components (including containment) are part of the certified design, thus connecting the referenced material selection to the certified containment design scope.

Section A identifies the certified design boundaries and components, while Section B specifies the applicable ASME code requirements for material selection and construction, connecting the design scope to the regulatory standards that must be met.

Section A and Section B both discuss the interface requirements between the certified design and the site-specific design, with Section B listing the combined license information items referenced in Section A.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME and NRC code requirements for design and construction, while Section B identifies which structures, systems, and components (including containment) are part of the certified design, thus connecting the regulatory basis for material selection (A) to the scope of containment design (B).

Section A lists regulatory guides and criteria with which the design claims conformance, including references to sections on materials (5.2) and quality assurance, while Section B details conformance with specific Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria related to system functions, indicating how the design addresses or partially addresses these requirements; thus, Section A provides a structural overview of regulatory conformance that includes the material selection discussed in Section B.

Section A references Section B by listing ASME NQA-1 (cited in Section B) as a quality assurance standard that the design conforms to, thereby connecting the regulatory conformance statement to the referenced material standards.

Section A references the quality assurance program and its conformance with regulatory guides, while Section B lists the topical reports (including the quality assurance program) that are incorporated by reference, thus Section A cites and relies on the material referenced in Section B to demonstrate regulatory conformance.

Section A describes the vacuum containment vessel's material-related benefits for corrosion and leakage control, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for process and post-accident sampling systems, with partial conformance noted; thus, Section A's material selection is intended to support containment design, but Section B only partially addresses related regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the vacuum environment in the CNV and its corrosion-mitigating benefits, while Section B lists material standards and guidelines (such as EPRI and ASME) that inform the selection of materials suitable for such an environment, thus connecting material selection to containment design requirements.

Section A discusses the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which reduces corrosion risks and supports material reliability, while Section B lists referenced reports (including those on quality assurance) that provide the technical basis for material selection, thus Section B supports the claims made in Section A about material adequacy.

Section A describes the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which relies on material selection to minimize corrosion and leakage, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for systems related to containment and sampling, indicating that material choices must support these safety and regulatory requirements.

Section A describes the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which reduces corrosion risks, while Section B references industry standards and guidelines (such as EPRI and ASME) that inform the selection of materials resistant to corrosion and stress cracking, thus connecting the environmental design condition (vacuum) to the material requirements.

Section A describes the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which are dependent on material properties such as corrosion resistance, while Section B lists the referenced reports (including those on material selection and quality assurance) that provide the technical basis for those material choices.

Section A describes the materials used for the reactor vessel and their role in the containment system, while Section B lists references including industry guidelines and standards (such as EPRI chemistry guidelines and ASME NQA-1) that provide the regulatory and technical basis for evaluating the adequacy of those material selections.

Section A describes the reactor vessel materials used in the containment design, while Section B lists the referenced topical reports (including those on material quality assurance) that provide the technical basis for the material selection in Section A.

Section A describes the physical configuration and components of the reactor vessel and containment, while Section B outlines regulatory acceptance criteria for systems related to containment and sampling, connecting them through the need for material selection in Section A to satisfy the regulatory requirements in Section B.

Section A describes the reactor vessel and its containment context, while Section B lists references including material reliability and quality assurance standards that are used to justify or support the adequacy of material selection for the containment design.

Section A describes the reactor vessel and its containment context, while Section B lists the referenced topical reports (including those on material selection and quality assurance) that provide the technical basis for the materials used in the containment design, thereby supporting the adequacy of material selection for containment.

Section B explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV minimizes moisture and corrosion risk, which supports the material selection criteria referenced in Section A's conformance with regulatory guides for quality assurance and materials (notably Section 5.2), thus linking the operational environment to the adequacy of material selection for containment design.

Section B explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV minimizes moisture and corrosion risk, which directly supports the material selection criteria referenced in Section A for ensuring containment integrity.

Section A references regulatory conformance requirements, including those for materials and quality assurance, while Section B describes the actual materials used for the reactor vessel, allowing assessment of whether the material selection satisfies the regulatory requirements cited in Section A.

Section A references Section 5.2, which contains material specifications relevant to the containment vessel, while Section B describes the reactor vessel and its relationship to the containment vessel, connecting the regulatory conformance in A to the design details in B.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, both referencing how this design feature minimizes corrosion and supports material reliability, thus connecting material selection to containment design requirements.

Section A explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV minimizes moisture and corrosion risk, while Section B describes the materials used for the reactor vessel and containment, connecting the material selection to the environmental conditions described in Section A.

Section A explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV minimizes moisture and corrosion risk, which is directly relevant to the material selection described in Section B for the reactor vessel and containment vessel, supporting the adequacy of those materials for the containment design.

Section A explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV reduces moisture and corrosion risk, while Section B describes the materials used for the reactor vessel and containment, connecting the material selection to the vacuum environment's corrosion mitigation benefits.

Section A explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV minimizes moisture and corrosion risks, while Section B describes the materials and structure of the reactor vessel and containment, connecting the vacuum environment's safety benefits to the need for appropriate material selection to prevent corrosion and ensure integrity.

Section A details compliance with material and design codes for reactor coolant boundary components, while Section B discusses conformance with regulatory criteria for systems related to containment and sampling, connecting them through the regulatory requirements that material selection must satisfy for containment design.

Section A asserts compliance with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements for material selection, while Section B lists references including EPRI and ASME NQA-1 standards that provide supporting technical and quality assurance guidance for those material choices, thus Section B supports the claims made in Section A regarding adequacy of material selection.

Section A asserts compliance with regulatory codes for material selection in pressure boundary components, while Section B lists the referenced reports (including those on quality assurance) that provide supporting documentation for these material choices, thus Section B supports the claims made in Section A.

Section A discusses the design features and material selection for maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for systems controlling radioactive releases, with Section A's material choices needing to satisfy the requirements implied by Section B for adequate containment design.

Section A describes the design features and integrity requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B lists references (such as EPRI and ASME standards) that provide the technical basis and guidelines for material selection supporting those integrity requirements.

Section A describes the design features required to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B lists the referenced topical reports (including those on quality assurance) that provide supporting documentation for material selection and design compliance, thus Section B supports the claims made in Section A regarding material adequacy.

Section A discusses the applicability of water chemistry and pH requirements for containment materials in the NuScale design, while Section B addresses the applicability of sampling and drainage system requirements, both referencing how specific regulatory criteria apply or do not apply to the NuScale containment and its materials.

Section A discusses the applicability of water chemistry and material corrosion requirements to the NuScale containment design, while Section B lists references (including materials reliability and chemistry guidelines) that provide the technical basis for evaluating whether the selected materials meet those requirements.

Section A discusses the partial conformance of the NuScale design with regulatory criteria regarding water chemistry and material corrosion, while Section B lists the referenced materials and topical reports that provide supporting technical details for the design, indicating that the referenced materials in Section B are intended to support the claims and compliance discussed in Section A.

Section A references the technical adequacy of probabilistic risk assessment and human factors for risk-informed licensing, while Section B addresses equipment and drainage systems for controlling radioactive releases; both sections are connected through their conformance with regulatory criteria in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, but neither directly addresses material selection for containment design, making their relationship only semantically related in the context of the analysis question.

Section B provides references and standards for material selection and quality assurance, which are necessary to evaluate whether the materials listed in Table 5.2-4 meet the containment design requirements specified in Table 1.2-2, thus supporting the adequacy of the design as discussed in Section A.

Section A discusses the conformance of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report with regulatory criteria, while Section B lists the referenced materials and topical reports (including those related to quality assurance) that support the technical basis for the containment design, thus Section B provides supporting evidence for the claims made in Section A.

Section A discusses conformance with regulatory criteria for protecting systems from fluid instabilities (GDC 4), while Section B addresses control of radioactive releases and sampling systems, both referencing how the NuScale design partially conforms to standard regulatory requirements, thus both sections provide evidence supporting the overall regulatory acceptability of the containment and associated systems.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for material selection to prevent flow accelerated corrosion in the condensate and feedwater system, while Section B lists references (such as EPRI and ASME standards) that provide technical guidance and requirements for material integrity, thus Section B supports the claims and conformance described in Section A.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and partial conformance for system design, while Section B lists the referenced materials and topical reports that provide supporting technical details, indicating that Section B supports the claims or requirements outlined in Section A.

Section A discusses the need for operating procedures to maintain reactor vessel integrity within pressure-temperature limits, while Section B lists references including material reliability guidelines that inform the selection and qualification of materials used in the reactor coolant boundary, thereby supporting the containment design.

Section A discusses the need for operating procedures to maintain reactor vessel integrity by adhering to pressure-temperature limits, while Section B lists the referenced materials and topical reports (such as quality assurance) that provide the technical basis for the design and procedures described in Section A.

Section A specifies that the reactor coolant pressure boundary components comply with ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a, which directly supports Section B's claim that the design features maintain RCPB integrity as defined by regulatory requirements.

Section A describes compliance with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements for material selection in pressure-retaining components, while Section B discusses partial conformance with BTP 6-1 water chemistry requirements, both addressing aspects of material suitability for containment design under regulatory criteria.

Section A demonstrates compliance with regulatory codes for material selection in pressure-retaining components, which is necessary to satisfy the containment design requirements referenced in Section B.

Section A describes compliance with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements for material selection in pressure-retaining components, while Section B outlines the regulatory acceptance criteria (GDC 4) for protecting systems from fluid instabilities, thus Section A's material compliance is intended to satisfy the regulatory requirements described in Section B.

Section A establishes that the reactor pressure vessel and associated components are designed and constructed in compliance with ASME BPVC requirements, while Section B states that operating procedures will ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, together indicating that material selection (from Table 5.2-4) supports the containment design requirements (from Table 1.2-2) by both design and operational controls.

Section A describes the design features and material selection for maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria for water chemistry and material compatibility, connecting them through the requirement that selected materials must withstand the chemical environment during emergency conditions.

Section A describes the design features and material requirements for maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and acceptance standards that the design and material selection must satisfy, connecting them through the need for technical adequacy and regulatory conformance.

Section A describes the design features ensuring the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria (GDC 4) for protecting systems from fluid instabilities, both of which are connected through the requirement that material selection must support both pressure boundary integrity and resistance to flow-induced degradation.

Section A outlines the design features and regulatory definitions ensuring the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B describes the development of operating procedures to maintain reactor vessel integrity by adhering to pressure-temperature limits, thus both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining system integrity via material selection and operational controls.

Section A discusses the applicability of regulatory criteria for water chemistry and material corrosion in the NuScale containment, while Section B addresses regulatory criteria for material protection against flow-accelerated corrosion in the condensate and feedwater system; both sections are related through their evaluation of material selection and corrosion control as required by regulatory standards.

Section A discusses the chemical and material compatibility requirements (such as pH and corrosion concerns) for containment systems, while Section B addresses the need for operating procedures to maintain reactor vessel integrity under specified conditions; both sections are connected through the necessity for material selection and operational controls to ensure containment and vessel safety under design and transient conditions.

Section A outlines the general regulatory criteria and acceptance standards for technical adequacy and conformance, while Section B provides specific requirements and partial conformance details for material selection in the condensate and feedwater system, connecting them through the need for material choices to meet regulatory design and safety criteria.

Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by not exceeding pressure-temperature limits, which relies on the material properties specified in Table 5.2-4 (materials) referenced in Section A, thus connecting material selection to containment design adequacy.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for protecting systems from fluid instabilities and corrosion, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under specified conditions; both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining the structural integrity and safety of containment-related systems by addressing material performance and operational limits.

Section A details the material selection and regulatory compliance for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is a prerequisite for demonstrating that the containment design (referenced in Table 1.2-2) is adequately supported by appropriate materials, as required by regulatory criteria discussed in Section B.

Section A describes the material selection and compliance with ASME and regulatory requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B lists references (including ASME and EPRI documents) that provide the standards and guidelines supporting the material selection described in Section A.

Section A specifies that the material selection for the reactor coolant pressure boundary conforms to ASME and regulatory requirements, while Section B lists the topical reports (including those on quality assurance) that are referenced to support the design certification, thus Section A's claims are supported by the referenced documentation in Section B.

Section A discusses criteria for radiological consequences and containment integrity, while Section B addresses control of radioactive releases and sampling systems; both are related through their focus on ensuring containment effectiveness and radiological safety, but they address different aspects (design criteria vs. operational controls) that together support the overall containment design.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria for containment integrity and radiological consequences, while Section B lists the material standards and references that are used to ensure the reactor coolant boundary's integrity, thus the material selection in Section B is intended to satisfy the containment design requirements specified in Section A.

Section A references regulatory criteria for containment and cladding integrity, while Section B lists the topical and technical reports (including those on material selection) that are incorporated by reference to support the containment design, thus Section A relies on the material information provided in Section B to demonstrate regulatory conformance.

Section A discusses the partial conformance of the containment design with fission product control system criteria, while Section B addresses the partial conformance of sampling and drainage systems for radioactive material control, both referencing how specific regulatory requirements apply or do not apply to the NuScale containment design.

Section A discusses the partial conformance of the containment design to regulatory criteria, while Section B lists references (including materials reliability and quality assurance standards) that provide the technical basis for material selection supporting the containment design.

Section A discusses the partial conformance of the containment design with regulatory criteria, while Section B lists the referenced materials and reports (including those on material selection) that support the design's compliance, thus Section B provides supporting documentation for the claims made in Section A.

Section A discusses the containment design features and their conformance with regulatory criteria, while Section B addresses the control of radioactive releases and sampling systems, both referencing aspects of the NuScale containment system and its compliance with specific SRP criteria, thus connecting through their evaluation of containment-related safety requirements.

Section A discusses the containment design features and regulatory conformance, while Section B lists material standards and references that provide the technical basis for material selection, thereby supporting the adequacy of the containment design.

Section A discusses the regulatory acceptance criteria for containment systems, while Section B lists the referenced materials and topical reports (including those on material selection) that provide the technical basis for the containment design, thus Section B supports the claims made in Section A regarding regulatory conformance.

Section A details the material selection and regulatory compliance for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is essential for ensuring the containment design (referenced in Section B) can maintain cladding integrity and meet radiological exposure guidelines in the event of a loss of containment vacuum.

Section A provides evidence that the selected materials for the reactor coolant pressure boundary conform to ASME and regulatory requirements, which supports Section B's claim of partial conformance with primary containment regulatory criteria.

Section A details the material selection and regulatory compliance for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is a prerequisite for evaluating whether the containment design in Section B (which references regulatory criteria for containment structures) is adequately supported by those materials.

Section A references regulatory criteria for cladding integrity and radiological consequences, while Section B specifies acceptance criteria for primary containment structures, both of which are connected through the requirement that material selection (from Table 5.2-4) must ensure containment integrity under accident conditions as described in the containment design (Table 1.2-2).

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria for radiological consequences and cladding integrity in containment design, while Section B addresses the acceptance criteria for containment structures and fission product control, both of which are connected through their requirements for material selection to ensure containment integrity and radiological safety.

Section A and Section B both discuss the NuScale containment vessel's partial conformance with SRP 6.5.3, specifically noting the absence of fission product clean-up and pressure suppression systems, thus providing parallel regulatory context for evaluating material selection in relation to containment design.
